title: How much honey to take
summary: Two beekeepers in New Zealand disagree about how much honey to take from the hive before winter.

body.speaker: Ruth
body.text: The hives are heavy this year. We can take a lot of honey.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: We can. But how much will you leave for the bees?

body.speaker: Ruth
body.text: Enough. I always leave enough. But this year the price is high. We should take more.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: The bees made that honey for the winter, not for us. If we take too much, they will be hungry in July.

body.speaker: Ruth
body.text: We can feed them sugar water. Many farmers do this.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: Sugar water is not honey. It keeps them alive, yes. But honey has many things in it. The bees are weaker on sugar. I have seen it.

body.speaker: Ruth
body.text: So you want to take less and earn less?

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: I want to take less this year and have strong bees next year. A strong hive gives honey for ten years. A weak hive dies in two.

body.speaker: Ruth
body.text: That is a long way to think.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: Bees think in years, not in one season. If we copy them, we do well too.

body.speaker: Ruth
body.text: My father took all the honey every year.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: And how many hives did he lose each winter?

body.speaker: Ruth
body.text: Some. A few. Maybe more than a few.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: We do not count the dead hives in the price of the honey. But they are part of the price.

body.speaker: Ruth
body.text: Fine. We take half this year. If the bees are strong in spring, you were right.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: If they are strong in spring, the bees were right. We only listened.

grammarNotes.title: First conditional: if + present, will + verb
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu điều kiện loại 1 nói về kết quả có thể xảy ra trong tương lai: 'If + hiện tại đơn, ... will + động từ.' Trong bài: 'If we take too much, they will be hungry in July.' Mệnh đề 'if' dùng hiện tại đơn (take), mệnh đề kết quả dùng 'will'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The first conditional talks about a likely future result: 'If + present simple, ... will + verb.' From the dialogue: 'If we take too much, they will be hungry in July.' The 'if' clause uses the present simple (take); the result clause uses 'will'.

translationVi:
Ruth: Năm nay các tổ ong nặng lắm. Mình có thể lấy nhiều mật.
Sam: Có thể. Nhưng cậu để lại bao nhiêu cho ong?
Ruth: Đủ chứ. Tớ luôn để lại đủ. Nhưng năm nay giá cao. Mình nên lấy nhiều hơn.
Sam: Ong làm mật đó cho mùa đông, không phải cho mình. Nếu lấy quá nhiều, tháng Bảy chúng sẽ đói.
Ruth: Mình cho chúng ăn nước đường được. Nhiều người nuôi ong làm vậy.
Sam: Nước đường không phải mật. Đúng là nó giúp chúng sống. Nhưng mật có nhiều thứ trong đó. Ăn đường thì ong yếu hơn. Tớ thấy rồi.
Ruth: Vậy cậu muốn lấy ít hơn và kiếm ít hơn à?
Sam: Tớ muốn năm nay lấy ít hơn và sang năm có đàn ong khỏe. Một tổ khỏe cho mật mười năm. Một tổ yếu chết trong hai năm.
Ruth: Cách nghĩ xa thật.
Sam: Ong nghĩ theo năm, không theo một vụ. Nếu mình bắt chước chúng, mình cũng được lợi.
Ruth: Bố tớ năm nào cũng lấy hết mật.
Sam: Thế mỗi mùa đông ông mất bao nhiêu tổ?
Ruth: Vài tổ. Một ít. Có khi hơn một ít.
Sam: Mình không tính những tổ chết vào giá mật. Nhưng chúng là một phần của cái giá.
Ruth: Được. Năm nay lấy một nửa thôi. Nếu mùa xuân ong khỏe, là cậu đúng.
Sam: Nếu mùa xuân ong khỏe, là ong đúng. Mình chỉ lắng nghe thôi.

questions.prompt: Why does Ruth want to take a lot of honey this year?
options: The hives are heavy and the price is high
questions.explanation: 'The hives are heavy this year' and 'this year the price is high.'

questions.prompt: Who did the bees make the honey for, according to Sam?
options: For the winter, not for the beekeepers
questions.explanation: 'The bees made that honey for the winter, not for us.'

questions.prompt: What does Ruth suggest giving the bees instead?
What does Sam say about bees on sugar water?
questions.explanation: 'The bees are weaker on sugar. I have seen it.'

questions.prompt: How long does a strong hive give honey, in Sam's view?
questions.explanation: 'A strong hive gives honey for ten years.'
questions.hint: His comparison of strong and weak hives.

questions.prompt: What did Ruth's father do every year?
questions.explanation: 'My father took all the honey every year.'

questions.prompt: What happened to her father's hives in winter?
options: He lost some, maybe more than a few
questions.explanation: 'Some. A few. Maybe more than a few.'

questions.prompt: What does Sam mean: the dead hives 'are part of the price'?
options: Taking all the honey has a hidden cost: lost hives
questions.explanation: 'We do not count the dead hives in the price... But they are part of the price.'
questions.hint: Inference about cost; near the end.

options: Taking less now can mean more over many years — thinking long, like the bees.
Sugar water is best for bees.
questions.explanation: Sam argues for the long view: strong bees over years.

cloze.template:

Sam: We can. But how much will you leave for the {{b2}}?
Ruth: Enough. I always leave enough. But this year the {{b3}} is high. We should take more.
Sam: The bees made that honey for the {{b4}}, not for us. If we take too much, they will be {{b5}} in July.
Ruth: We can feed them sugar water. Many farmers do this.
Sam: Sugar water is not honey. It keeps them alive, yes. But honey has many things in it. The bees are {{b6}} on sugar. I have seen it.
Ruth: So you want to take less and earn less?
Sam: I want to take less this year and have strong bees next year. A strong hive gives honey for {{b7}} years. A weak hive dies in two.
Ruth: That is a long way to think.
Sam: Bees think in years, not in one {{b8}}. If we copy them, we do well too.
Ruth: My father took all the honey every year.
Sam: And how many hives did he {{b9}} each winter?
Ruth: Some. A few. Maybe more than a few.

Ruth: Fine. We take half this year. If the bees are strong in spring, you were right.
Sam: If they are strong in spring, the bees were right. We only listened.

blanks.explanation: How many hives did he lose.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Sam says the dead hives are part of the price, even though no one counts them. Where in your own choices is there a hidden cost that does not show up right away — in study, money, or rest — that you could choose to count?
